title: Advisory Trending and New Advisory Detection

compliance.assurance_rationale: Tracks Cisco PSIRT security advisories affecting managed devices, providing evidence of vulnerability awareness and remediation tracking as required by NIST 800-53 Flaw Remediation.
compliance.controlObjective: Demonstrates continuous monitoring of vendor-published security advisories for all managed network infrastructure.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search over index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:securityadvisory scheduled daily; advisory counts and affected device lists exported to compliance dashboard.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Contributes to vulnerability scanning by tracking known vendor-published vulnerabilities, though it does not replace active scanning tools.
compliance.controlObjective: Provides a vendor-authoritative vulnerability feed for network infrastructure to complement active vulnerability scanning.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Dashboard panel showing advisory severity distribution and affected device counts, refreshed with each TA poll cycle.

dataSources: `index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:securityadvisory` from the `securityadvisory` modular input in `TA_cisco_catalyst` (Splunkbase 7538). Same data feed as UC-5.13.34. For trending, retain **90+ days** to show advisory exposure over time.
spl:
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:securityadvisory"
| where deviceCount > 0
| timechart span=1w dc(advisoryId) as active_advisories by severity
description: Tracks the number and severity distribution of active security advisories over time, revealing whether the vulnerability exposure is growing (new advisories outpacing remediation), shrinking (firmware campaigns reducing exposure), or stable — the trend that drives the quarterly vulnerability management review.
value: UC-5.13.34 shows today's advisory snapshot. This UC shows the trajectory. A fleet with 15 active advisories today is fine if it was 30 last month (remediation is working). It's alarming if it was 5 last month (new exposures outpacing patches). The trend line answers the executive question: 'Is our vulnerability posture improving or degrading?' and proves that firmware investment is actually reducing exposure. For NIST RA-5, it demonstrates ongoing vulnerability scanning and remediation as a continuous process.
implementation: Same `securityadvisory` input as UC-5.13.34. Retain 90+ days. Use `timechart span=1w dc(advisoryId)` for weekly trending.
visualization: (1) Stacked area chart: `dc(advisoryId)` by severity over time (CRITICAL red, HIGH orange, MEDIUM yellow). (2) Line overlay: total affected device count trend. (3) Stat panel: advisory count this month vs last month vs 3 months ago. (4) Annotations: mark firmware campaign dates to show remediation impact.
knownFalsePositives:
**PSIRT database update generating apparent new advisory detections.** When Catalyst Center updates its PSIRT database, previously unknown advisories may appear for the first time even though the vulnerabilities are not new. Distinguish by checking the advisory's `publicationDate` — if it was published months ago but just detected, the PSIRT database was recently updated. Suppress by alerting on advisories where `publicationDate` is within the last 30 days as genuinely new, and treating older publications as database-update catches.

**Advisory reclassification changing severity between polls.** Cisco may update the severity of a published PSIRT (e.g., upgrading from HIGH to CRITICAL after exploitation is confirmed). Catalyst Center reflects this change. Distinguish by checking whether the `severity` changed for the same `advisoryId` between consecutive polls. Do not suppress — severity upgrades are operationally important.

**Same advisory appearing for different device UUIDs as more devices are scanned.** The initial scan may not cover all devices; subsequent polls include additional affected devices for the same advisory. Distinguish by tracking `dc(deviceId)` per `advisoryId` over time — an increasing count is normal as the scan completes. Suppress by waiting for the full scan to complete (typically 24 hours) before considering the advisory trending stable.

**Advisory with zero affected devices remaining in the feed.** After all affected devices are patched, the advisory may remain in the feed with `deviceCount=0` until Catalyst Center removes it. Distinguish by checking `deviceCount=0`. Suppress by filtering `| where deviceCount>0` for active advisory dashboards.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• UC-5.13.34 (PSIRT Overview) must be operational — same data feed.
• Retain **90+ days** of advisory data for meaningful trending. Advisory data is lightweight (~2.7 MB/day) so long retention is affordable.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Same `securityadvisory` input as UC-5.13.34. No additional configuration.

Step 2 — Create the search and dashboard panel
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:securityadvisory"
| where deviceCount > 0
| timechart span=1w dc(advisoryId) as active_advisories by severity
```

Why `where deviceCount > 0`: counts only advisories that affect your fleet. Advisories with no affected devices are noise in the trend.

Why `span=1w`: weekly granularity smooths daily poll timing differences and produces clean lines for monthly/quarterly views.

Why `dc(advisoryId)`: counts unique advisories per week. An advisory that persists across 4 weeks shows as 1 per week, not 4. The trend shows the active exposure count, not the cumulative discovery count.

For remediation rate analysis:
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:securityadvisory"
| stats earliest(_time) as first_seen latest(_time) as last_seen latest(deviceCount) as current_affected by advisoryId, severity
| eval active=if(current_affected > 0, 1, 0)
| eval remediated=if(current_affected = 0, 1, 0)
| stats sum(active) as active sum(remediated) as remediated by severity
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Run the trend over 90 days. The shape should reflect your organisation's firmware upgrade cadence: dips after upgrade campaigns, spikes when Cisco publishes new advisories.

(b) Compare the most recent week's `active_advisories` with UC-5.13.34's current count. They should match.

(c) Cross-reference firmware campaign dates with dips in the trend to validate cause-effect.

Step 4 — Operationalize
• Quarterly vulnerability review: show the 90-day trend to demonstrate remediation progress (NIST RA-5 / SI-2 evidence).
• Annotate firmware campaign dates on the chart.
• Track month-over-month: is the CRITICAL count trending down?

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **Trend shows steady increase** — new Cisco advisories are being published faster than you're patching. Increase firmware upgrade cadence.

• **Trend shows sudden spike** — Cisco published a batch of advisories (common during quarterly security updates). Check the Cisco PSIRT portal for the publication batch.

• **Trend shows sudden drop to 0** — either all advisories were remediated (verify) or the `securityadvisory` input stopped running (check `index=_internal`).

• **`deviceCount > 0` filter removes all data** — your devices may not be affected by any current advisories. This is the ideal state.

• **Advisory count in the trend disagrees with Catalyst Center** — time window and deduplication differences. Align for comparison.

• **Trend doesn't show remediation impact after firmware push** — the advisory-device mapping updates within 24 hours of firmware upgrade. Wait for the next advisory poll after the upgrade.

• **`severity` values changed** — Cisco may reclassify advisory severity. This shifts events between series in the chart.

• **No data for some weeks** — data gap in the `securityadvisory` input. Check `index=_internal` for errors during those weeks.
grandmaExplanation: We track how many security weaknesses affect your network over time — week by week, month by month. When the number goes down after your team patches devices, you can prove the investment worked. When it goes up, you know new vulnerabilities are appearing faster than fixes.
